mnemonics.title: E-Waste Management Steps
mnemonics.content: CHoPS+D: Collection → Handling → Processing → Separation → Disposal/Recovery + Documentation

mnemonics.title: Hazardous Elements in E-waste
mnemonics.content: Lions (Lead) May (Mercury) Crawl (Cadmium) Carefully (Chromium) Over (Arsenic) Dirty (Dioxins) Surfaces (Sb/Antimony)

acronyms.usage: Policy making producers responsible for end-of-life product management
Nodal agency for e-waste management in India
Old display technology containing lead and mercury

visualTips.visualization: Draw a flowchart: Collection → Transportation → Storage → Dismantling → Shredding → Magnetic Separation → Eddy Current Separation → Refining → Raw Material Recovery
Mental image of a computer: Monitor (lead/mercury) → Circuit boards (lead/tin) → Batteries (cadmium) → Capacitors (PCB oils) → Cables (copper PVC)
Pyramid with Reduce at top (most preferred), Reuse in middle, Recycle at base (least preferred but still important)

analogies.analogy: Just as a mine extracts precious metals from the earth, e-waste contains valuable resources like gold and copper that can be 'mined' through recycling.
Like parents responsible for their children, producers are responsible for their products throughout their lifecycle including disposal.
Just as medical waste is segregated into infectious, pathological, and general, e-waste must separate hazardous from non-hazardous components.
